data.title: 3 things to look out for on Friday
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market closed markedly higher on Thursday as investors assessed a slew of economic data, including the key PCE and GDP reports, in addition to a string of corporate earnings reports.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was +1.6%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +1.1% and reached an intraday record, and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.9%.

Now, here are three focus points for investors on Friday:

Chevron (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) head into Q1 earnings with expectations of double-digit profit declines, while investor attention sharpens on guidance against a volatile backdrop shaped in part by ongoing Middle East supply and geopolitical risks, as oil majors invest billions in new energy sources. For Exxon, the consensus EPS estimate is $1.01 (-42.6% Y/Y) and the consensus revenue estimate is $85.29B (+2.6% Y/Y). CVX, meanwhile, is expected to post a profit of $0.97 a share (-55.5% Y/Y) on revenue of $52.7B (+10.7% Y/Y).

The big thing to watch on the economic calendar is the April manufacturing read-through, especially new orders, prices paid, supplier delivery times and employment. A softer activity print with firmer prices would reinforce the uncomfortable stagflationary setup that has been pressuring the rate-cut story. The ISM Manufacturing PMI is expected to edge up to 53.1 from 52.7 in March, according to the average economist estimate.

The separate S&P Global final U.S. Manufacturing PMI is set to hit right before ISM, so it can shape the first read on the health of U.S. manufacturing. It's expected to hold steady at 54.0 (a reading above 50 indicates expansion).

data.title: Darling Ingredients anticipates Q2 core ingredients EBITDA of $260M-$275M as RVO shifts renewables margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Darling Ingredients (DAR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Headwinds have now shifted, and the results we share today confirm a much more favorable operating environment" (Chairman & CEO Randall Stuewe). "For the first quarter of 2026... Combined adjusted EBITDA for first quarter was $406.8 million, including $255.6 million from our global ingredients business and $151.2 million from Diamond Green Diesel" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).
  • "Our Feed Ingredients segment had a fantastic quarter" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe). "The renewable volume obligation announced at the end of March has been extremely constructive for Darling and DGD" and "we anticipate this to be a nice tailwind for our Feed segment for the remainder of 2026" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).
  • "We are seeing nice growth in collagen, particularly in Europe and Asia" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe). "Our Nextida glucose control product is currently pending a patent... offering a nonpharmaceutical option targeting lower blood glucose" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).
  • "For the quarter, combined adjusted EBITDA was $407 million, versus $196 million in first quarter 2025 and $336 million last quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Robert Day). "Overall, net income was approximately $134 million for the quarter or $0.83 per diluted share" (Executive VP & CFO Day).
  • "Those asset sales continue to move forward but have not yet closed" and "we have signed an agreement to sell the majority of our grease trap environmental service assets" (Executive VP & CFO Day).

Outlook

  • "This confidence is reflected in our core ingredients EBITDA guidance for Q2, which we are now setting at $260 million to $275 million for the quarter" (Chairman & CEO Randall Stuewe).
  • "We are willing to say that we think that second quarter at DGD will be stronger than the first quarter" and "we did say we expect 320 million gallons for the quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Robert Day).
  • "The ingredients business will have a stronger Q2" and "that will start to flow through very nicely in Q2" as fat prices accelerated into March (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).

Financial Results

  • "Total net sales were $1.6 billion" and "gross margins for the quarter improved to 26.1%" (Executive VP & CFO Robert Day).
  • "Looking at the Feed segment for the quarter, EBITDA improved to $169 million" and "gross margins relative to sales improved nicely to 25.3%" (Executive VP & CFO Day).
  • "In the Food segment, total sales for the quarter were $405 million" and "EBITDA for first quarter 2026 was $81 million" (Executive VP & CFO Day).
  • "Darling's share of DGD EBITDA for the quarter was $151 million, which includes a favorable LCM inventory valuation adjustment of $97 million at the DGD entity level" and "sales of around 272 million gallons" (Executive VP & CFO Day).
  • "As of quarter-end, total debt net of cash was approximately $4 billion" and "capital expenditures totaled $95 million" (Executive VP & CFO Day).

Q&A

  • Heather Jones, Heather Jones Research LLC: asked if "the hedging and LIFO losses" and "LCM" reverse; Executive VP & CFO Day: "that $97 million... exhausts all available lower of cost or market" and "we do not anticipate any lower of cost or market benefits" going forward.
  • Thomas Palmer, JPMorgan: asked whether U.S. biofuels capacity can meet the RVO; Executive VP & CFO Day: "we think that the industry is capable of meeting the mandate... of the RVO" and it "will include some imports of fuel".
  • Pooran Sharma, Stephens: asked about diesel tightness; Executive VP & CFO Day: "we're not really qualified" on diesel constraints, but said higher energy prices are "allowing margins in renewable fuels to sort of move... more quickly".
  • Manav Gupta, UBS: asked about "50% RIN on foreign feedstock"; Executive VP & CFO Day: "overall, we don't see it as a negative" and said outcomes depend on tariffs and global feedstock dynamics.
  • Derrick Whitfield, Texas Capital: asked how higher waste FOG/fat realizations flow through; Chairman & CEO Stuewe: "you will start to see a lot of that flow through" and called core guidance "very conservative right now".
  • Dushyant Ailani, Jefferies: asked about weak LCFS pricing; Executive VP & CFO Day: "we think LCFS credit premiums are going to increase" and described California’s demand constraints versus mandated production.
  • Ben Kallo, Baird: asked about the collagen JV and peptides; Executive VP & CFO Day: "we're in an antitrust review process" and "we've never been more excited about the potential of forming that joint venture" with PB Leiner/Tessenderlo.
  • Conor Fitzpatrick, BofA: asked why biodiesel utilization lags and feedstocks; Chairman & CEO Stuewe: "restarting those plants coming out of winter just takes a little bit of time"; Executive VP & CFO Day: independents "don't have access to the production tax credit, practically speaking".

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on policy/credit mechanics and timing risk, including "volatility" and whether biodiesel "appears to be lagging" (Conor Fitzpatrick, BofA Securities).
  • Slightly positive management tone, emphasizing improved conditions and visibility from policy, including "we expect DGD's results to continue to strengthen throughout the year" (Chairman & CEO Randall Stuewe).
  • More confident than the prior quarter on policy impact, with management moving from "we are increasingly optimistic" (Q4) to "Fuel segment is at an inflection point" (Q1) (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The RVO shifted from anticipation in Q4 ("we're reconsider" guiding DGD "after we get a final ruling on the RVO") to Q1 claiming the March RVO was "extremely constructive" and that renewables margins "turned a corner" (Executive VP & CFO Robert Day; Chairman & CEO Randall Stuewe).
  • Profitability improved sequentially, with Q1 combined adjusted EBITDA stated at $406.8 million vs. Q4 combined adjusted EBITDA stated at $336.1 million (Chairman & CEO Stuewe; Chairman & CEO Stuewe in Q4).
  • Analysts stayed focused on the same core themes (RVO, RINs, LCFS, feedstock costs), but Q1 added more detailed discussion of operating lags, tariffs’ impact in Brazil, and DGD’s near-max run rate at "320 million" gallons for Q2 (Chairman & CEO Stuewe; Executive VP & CFO Day).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Public policy uncertainty" and "volatile commodity markets" were cited as the recent backdrop, with management pointing to "strict, disciplined capital allocation" and "meaningful debt reduction" as mitigants (Chairman & CEO Randall Stuewe).
  • Tariffs were cited as an operational variable: "the tariffs have impacted Brazil pretty sharply" and required pricing-model adjustments that "takes 30 to 60 days" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).
  • DGD experienced operational disruption: "DGD overcame a shutdown at Port Arthur that briefly interrupted our supply chain" (Chairman & CEO Stuewe).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a turning point driven by the late-March RVO and improving renewables margins, with combined adjusted EBITDA of $406.8 million and stated momentum in both core ingredients and Diamond Green Diesel. Leadership set Q2 core ingredients EBITDA guidance at $260 million to $275 million, characterized that range as conservative given rising fat prices, and reiterated balance-sheet priority with asset sales in progress and an Investor Day planned for May 11 to outline a three-year road map.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Butterfly projects Q2 revenue of $27M-$31M while reaffirming 2026 revenue of $117M-$121M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Butterfly Network (BFLY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Joseph DeVivo said the company began 2026 with “another strong financial performance, 25% revenue growth, 69% gross margin and the lowest first quarter net loss since going public,” adding, “Our plan to become the leader in point-of-care ultrasound is firmly established.”

  • DeVivo highlighted a regulatory milestone: “We are excited to be the first company ever to earn FDA clearance of a blind sweep AI tool, which in 2 minutes can determine the age of a fetus,” and said the gestational age tool “is expected to unlock new relationships with ministries of health across developing countries.”

  • DeVivo updated commercial timelines for the home initiative: “We expect now our first commercial agreement to be signed in the first half of this year and begin training nurses to support patient scanning across our full state in the third quarter,” and said the model “can expand significantly across the United States in 2027.”

  • CFO John Doherty said Q1 execution reflected “double-digit growth in both our core and embedded businesses,” citing “the expected commercial launch in an initial state with a major U.S. direct care provider” and “execution of a large 7-figure TCV Compass AI contract.”

  • DeVivo positioned Embedded as a growth driver and said, “We signed our ninth partner this month and expect to add at least 2 more mid-year,” and called Midjourney progress “very promising.”

Outlook

  • Q2 2026 revenue guidance of $27M-$31M vs. analysts’ $28.94M.

  • Doherty guided Q2 adjusted EBITDA loss of $6M-$8M and said full-year 2026 guidance was reaffirmed, with revenue of $117M-$121M and adjusted EBITDA loss of $21M-$25M.

  • On assumptions and conservatism, Doherty said, “What’s in guidance now is everything that we have a line of sight of,” adding, “our posture is going to be a little bit more on the conservative side.”

Financial Results

  • Doherty reported Q1 2026 revenue of $26.5M, “an increase of 25% year-over-year,” and said the drivers included “strength in our U.S. health, international and vet channels,” mix shift to iQ3, and “Butterfly Embedded.”

  • Doherty introduced a core vs. embedded split and reported core revenue of $20.8M (up 10% year-over-year) and Butterfly Embedded revenue of $5.7M (up 147% year-over-year), saying the Embedded increase “was primarily driven by the Midjourney partnership.”

  • Doherty reported gross profit of $18.3M and gross margin of 69%, attributing margin to “the higher-margin Butterfly Embedded revenue and the higher mix of iQ3.”

  • Doherty reported adjusted EBITDA loss of $6.1M and said cash and cash equivalents (excluding restricted cash) ended at $138M, with “use of cash in the quarter” of $12.5M.

Q&A

  • Jacob Soucheray, Craig-Hallum: asked about med school momentum; CEO DeVivo said, “the second quarter is our biggest medical school quarter,” adding, “I’d expect our medical school activity to be very positive in the second quarter.”

  • Soucheray, Craig-Hallum: asked about Compass AI enterprise contribution; CEO DeVivo said the “pipeline is up pretty big,” and added, “I’d expect this to be a very good enterprise year. I’d expect this to be a very good Compass AI year.”

  • Joshua Jennings, TD Cowen: asked whether more Midjourney-like Embedded deals could emerge; CEO DeVivo said, “I would say yes,” and added, “there are conversations that we are having that could replicate a Midjourney type of transaction.”

  • Jennings, TD Cowen: asked how Home and Community Care scales; CEO DeVivo described the next phase as “our first commercial agreement,” and said, “I’d expect to see some revenue contribution in the second half of 2026” and expansion “more and more states” in 2027.

  • Andrew Brackmann, William Blair: asked what is included in guidance; CFO Doherty said, “the things that we have a very good line of sight on are in our guidance,” and pointed to investment needs: “with the launch of Home, it does require some investment to get it off the ground.”

  • Benjamin Haynor, Lake Street: asked about FDA clearance implications; CEO DeVivo said, “we have taken a longer path with the gestational AI tool,” adding, “when you get through that gauntlet, then you now have — you have a moat.”

  • Haynor, Lake Street: asked about form factors; CEO DeVivo said, “early next year, we will have a new probe,” and added the station remains on the roadmap: “early to mid-2027 time frame.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, emphasizing growth levers (Embedded pipeline, enterprise Compass AI, Home scaling) while pressing on visibility; Jennings asked whether investors should expect “more Midjourney type deals on tap,” and Brackmann asked what is “in guidance now” and the “potential upside lever.”

  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, with repeated forward-leaning language; CFO Doherty said, “We are very confident in where we’re going for 2026,” and CEO DeVivo said, “I’d expect this to be a very good enterprise year.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, management shifted from highlighting Q4’s record quarter and Midjourney validation to emphasizing FDA clearance, enterprise software TCV traction, and the operational ramp into Home and Community Care.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 reiterated the same full-year 2026 guidance ranges provided in Q4, while updating the near-term quarterly frame from Q4’s Q1 revenue guide of $24M-$28M to reported Q1 revenue of $26.5M and a new Q2 revenue guide of $27M-$31M.

  • Q1 added a new product/regulatory headline (FDA clearance for the gestational age blind sweep AI tool), while Q4 centered on the Midjourney deal and the rebrand to “Butterfly Embedded.”

  • Q1 provided more explicit business-model segmentation, with Doherty stating the company would provide a revenue split between “our core business and Butterfly Embedded,” and noting the split was included in the 10-Q.

Risks and Concerns

  • Doherty cited geopolitical and policy uncertainty: “We continue to monitor the war in the Middle East and the ripple effects on the global economy as well as tariffs in certain markets,” and said impacts “have been minor” and are reflected in guidance.

  • DeVivo discussed EU regulatory process uncertainty around lead-based materials, noting the reviewer “ultimately recommended reapproval of the exemption, but only for 2 years,” and said Butterfly expects the EU to “gain the understanding they need to revoke the lead exemption for handhelds.”

Final Takeaway

Butterfly’s management framed Q1 as a start-of-year execution quarter anchored by 25% revenue growth, a higher-margin mix supported by Embedded, and reaffirmed full-year 2026 guidance. DeVivo emphasized FDA clearance of a gestational age blind sweep AI tool as a platform catalyst for broader deployments, while reiterating timelines to sign a first Home and Community Care commercial agreement in the first half of 2026 and begin statewide nurse training in Q3, alongside continued Embedded partner expansion and enterprise Compass AI momentum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Roblox slashes revenue guidance as safety controls weigh on growth
data.description:
Roblox (RBLX) shares are losing ground in after-hours trading after the gaming platform’s first-quarter user metrics missed estimates and lowered FY26 revenue well below Wall Street’s expectations, citing the impact from safety measures.  

In the first quarter, revenue grew 39% year-over-year to $1.4B, and bookings were up 43% to $1.7B, although they were slightly below estimates of $1.42B and $1.73B, respectively.

Despite a considerable increase in revenue, a 31% increase in cost of revenue and higher developer exchange fees—reflecting the increase to creator earnings—drove the company further into the red with a loss of $0.35 per share from a loss of $0.32 in the same quarter last year. This was, however, 5 cents better than anticipated.

User metrics were mixed as daily active users grew by nearly 34M to 132M but was less than 143.8M estimates, while hours engaged were up 43% to 31B, shy of 33.7B estimates. DAU and bookings growth decelerated from the outsized gains experienced in 2025 along with the platform ban in Russia.

The company also believes DAU and hours engaged were impacted by greater-than-expected headwinds from age checks, which restricted communication for non-age-checked users, diluted communication for age-checked users, and slowed new user acquisition.

On the balance sheet, Roblox (RBLX) generated $629M in operating cash flow and $596M in free cash flow (versus $564.5M estimates), reflecting gains of 42% and 40%, respectively, year-over-year.

Looking ahead to the current quarter, Roblox (RBLX) expects revenue to be within a range of $1.39B and $1.45B, well below $1.90B estimates.

And for FY26, Roblox (RBLX) lowered its revenue guidance to reflect the continuation of the safety headwinds, now expected to increase by 20% to 25% to within a range of $5.878B and $6.14B versus previous guidance for growth of 23% to 29%. This compares to the consensus estimate of $8.42B.

Bookings growth was also lowered to 8% to 12% versus an increase of 22% to 26% previously.

data.title: LendingTree GAAP EPS of $1.22 beats by $0.31, revenue of $327.27M beats by $6.1M
data.description:
• LendingTree press release (TREE): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.22 beats by $0.31.
  • Revenue of $327.27M (+36.5% Y/Y) beats by $6.1M.

  • Financial Outlook*

    Today we are updating our full-year 2026 outlook and introducing our outlook for the second-quarter.

    Full-year 2026:

    • Revenue of $1,300 - $1,350 million vs consensus of $1.3B compared to the prior range of $1,275 - $1,330 million
    • Variable Marketing Margin of $378 - $395 million compared to $374 - $394 million previously
    • Adjusted EBITDA of $152 - $162 million versus the $150 - $160 million prior range

    Second-quarter 2026:

    • Revenue: $305 - $325 million vs consensus of $309.71M
    • Variable Marketing Margin: $93 - $97 million
    • Adjusted EBITDA: $38 - $40 million
  • Shares -5.34%.
